- The Dark Economics of OnlyFans: Why Its Success is Society's Failure: Kind: captions Language: en-US We all know the narrative. It's the ultimate story of digital empowerment. Back in 2016, a man named Tim Stokely took a 10000 pound loan from his father to build OnlyFans. The promise was simple and seductive: cut out the middlemen. Let creators connect directly with their fans and keep the lion's share of the profits. No studio gatekeepers and no corporate overlords. Just you, your audience, and your content. It sounded revolutionary. It sounded like freedom. But here's the thing about promises made in the digital age. They usually look a lot like a shiny new app, but operate a lot like an old school trap. OnlyFans bought into the idea of creator freedom, but what it actually built was a highly efficient machine for commodifying human desperation. Then came 2020. The world locked down, the economy fractured, and suddenly, millions of people were staring at a screen, wondering how to pay rent. OnlyFans exploded. We're talking 305 million registered users and over 4 million creators. Last year alone, over 7 billion dollars flowed through this system. The media loved it. They showed us the celebrities joining, like Cardi B and Bella Thorne. But behind the celebrity headlines, this was not a boom born of innovation. It was a boom born of necessity. When the traditional labor market fails, people look for lifeboats. And OnlyFans was waiting, algorithm primed, ready to turn that economic anxiety into a multi billion dollar revenue stream. Let's look at the actual math, because the numbers are brutal. OnlyFans sells us the dream of financial independence, but the reality is a wealth gap that makes Wall Street look like a socialist utopia. The top zero point one percent of OnlyFans creators are vacuuming up 76 percent of all the revenue. They earn an average of 146000 dollars a month. Meanwhile, the average creator is scraping by on about 150 dollars a month. That is not a typo, one hundred and fifty dollars. In fact, 87 percent of the people on this platform are making less than minimum wage. It's not a creator economy; it's a digital lottery where the house always wins. And the house, OnlyFans, takes 20 percent of every single dollar, whether you make ten bucks or ten million. The current owner paid himself 500 million dollars in dividends in just two years, while many creators could not make rent. But the real cost of OnlyFans isn't just financial, it's psychological. You do not commodify human intimacy at this scale without breaking something inside the people doing the selling. The data is starting to come out, and it reads like a clinical diagnosis: severe anxiety, depression, and profound shame. Over a third of OnlyFans creators, or 34 percent, report negative mental and physical health outcomes. That is not a fringe statistic, that is a public health signal. And the irony is almost poetic. On a platform built entirely around the illusion of connection and intimacy, the most common side effect is crushing loneliness. The transactional nature of OnlyFans seeps into every corner of a creator's life, isolating them from genuine relationships. We have built a system that demands constant, escalating vulnerability, and then leaves the creators to absorb the psychological fallout entirely on their own. And then we get to the darkest part of the OnlyFans undercurrent: the illusion of control. We're told OnlyFans creators are their own bosses. But investigations reveal a very different reality. Reports show that 30 percent of creators have been threatened with account deletion if they do not keep feeding the content machine. And an investigation from a formal NGO found that six percent had traffickers actively managing their accounts. Not managers. Traffickers. Then there is the manosphere pipeline. A growing network of so called OnlyFans management agencies are recruiting less experienced creators, taking massive cuts, and coercing increasingly extreme content. It is the oldest form of exploitation, just repackaged with a slick user interface and a subscription button. OnlyFans built the infrastructure. Society provided the desperation. The trap was always working exactly as designed. So here is the verdict. OnlyFans is not a success story. It is a symptom. It is the diagnostic readout of a society that has failed on multiple fronts simultaneously. OnlyFans thrives because our labor market is broken, because wages stagnated while the cost of living did not. It thrives because we have normalized exploitation as long as it is hidden behind a paywall and dressed up as empowerment. It thrives because we are increasingly isolated, atomized, and willing to pay for the illusion of human connection. Tim Stokely started OnlyFans with a 10000 pound loan and a dream of creator freedom. What it became is a billion dollar mirror held up to everything we have gotten wrong. We cannot just blame the platform. Society built the conditions that made OnlyFans not just possible, but inevitable. The question is not why it exists. The question is what it says about us that we let it. If you appreciate this kind of deep, unfiltered analysis, do me a favor, hit the like button and subscribe to Business Undercurrent. We are going to keep pulling back the curtain on the systems that actually run our world. Because the undercurrent always runs deep.

- The Terrifying Reality Behind OnlyFans' $7 Billion Empire: Kind: captions Language: en-US A man sits alone at his kitchen table somewhere&nbsp; in Illinois. He has been messaging the same woman&nbsp;&nbsp; for three weeks. Paying for conversations.&nbsp; Sharing things about his life. His job. His&nbsp;&nbsp; dog. His difficult relationship with&nbsp; his boss. He genuinely believes she&nbsp;&nbsp; listens. He genuinely believes she cares. Then one day, the messages start arriving&nbsp;&nbsp; with small inconsistencies. Details she should&nbsp; have remembered, forgotten. Responses that feel&nbsp;&nbsp; slightly off. He gets suspicious. He starts&nbsp; asking questions. The answers don't add up.&nbsp; He was never talking to her. He was talking to a paid employee in&nbsp;&nbsp; the Philippines, working from a script, trained&nbsp; to extract his emotional vulnerabilities and&nbsp;&nbsp; convert them into payment requests. Working&nbsp; from a detailed psychological profile built&nbsp;&nbsp; entirely from his previous messages. Using&nbsp; techniques that a lawsuit would later describe&nbsp;&nbsp; as specifically designed to "exploit emotional&nbsp; connections" and "prey on psychological biases."&nbsp; He was not the only one. There were hundreds&nbsp; of millions of users on the same platform,&nbsp;&nbsp; many of whom had no idea who they were&nbsp; actually talking to — or whether the&nbsp;&nbsp; person on the other end was even human at all. And the platform those interactions happened on&nbsp;&nbsp; generated $7.22 billion in a single year. The platform, of course, was OnlyFans.&nbsp; But here is the question&nbsp; nobody is asking loudly enough.&nbsp; How did a subscription website, launched&nbsp; from a family dining table in Essex, England,&nbsp;&nbsp; with a ten-thousand-pound loan from a retired&nbsp; banker, transform in less than a decade into&nbsp;&nbsp; one of the most profitable platforms in the&nbsp; history of the internet — and in doing so,&nbsp;&nbsp; turn human intimacy itself&nbsp; into an industrial product?&nbsp; That is the story we are about to tell. And&nbsp; it starts not with sex. But with a problem. Timothy Christopher Stokely was born in&nbsp; July 1983 in Harlow, Essex — a quiet,&nbsp;&nbsp; unremarkable market town northeast of London. He&nbsp; was the youngest of four children of Guy Stokely,&nbsp;&nbsp; a retired Barclays investment banker. He grew&nbsp; up comfortable, educated, and with a nose for&nbsp;&nbsp; opportunity that showed itself early. As a&nbsp; teenager, he collected takeaway orders from&nbsp;&nbsp; a local fish and chip shop and delivered them&nbsp; at a markup. The instinct was always the same:&nbsp;&nbsp; find where money is already flowing, position&nbsp; yourself in the middle of it, and take a cut.&nbsp; He graduated from Anglia Ruskin University&nbsp; with a degree in property and surveying.&nbsp;&nbsp; Then he went looking for the internet's&nbsp; equivalent of a fish and chip delivery route.&nbsp; He found it in adult content. Specifically,&nbsp;&nbsp; in the gap between what creators were&nbsp; producing and what platforms were keeping.&nbsp; In 2011, Stokely launched GlamWorship, a&nbsp; subscription site for adult content. Then&nbsp;&nbsp; Customs4U, a platform for commissioned custom&nbsp; content. Both gave him something invaluable:&nbsp;&nbsp; a deep understanding of how adult&nbsp; creators thought about money, audience,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the exhausting reality of depending on&nbsp; platforms that could demonetize, ban, or simply&nbsp;&nbsp; stop paying them without warning. He watched&nbsp; creators build audiences on free platforms,&nbsp;&nbsp; then hand most of the revenue to middlemen who&nbsp; controlled everything and owed them nothing.&nbsp; He identified the problem with surgical clarity.&nbsp; Creators needed a direct line to their audience.&nbsp;&nbsp; A platform that didn't take seventy or eighty&nbsp; percent. A system where the creator set the price,&nbsp;&nbsp; kept the relationship, and couldn't be&nbsp; wiped out by someone else's content policy.&nbsp; In November 2016, he launched OnlyFans. The&nbsp; initial investment was a £10,000 loan from his&nbsp;&nbsp; father — the same father who reportedly&nbsp; told him, "Tim, this is going to be the&nbsp;&nbsp; last one." His brother Thomas joined as Chief&nbsp; Operating Officer. His father became head of&nbsp;&nbsp; finance. OnlyFans launched as the ultimate family&nbsp; business — built around a genuinely radical idea.&nbsp; The idea was simple. Pay creators eighty percent.&nbsp; Let them keep their audience. Let them set their&nbsp;&nbsp; price. No advertisers. No middlemen.&nbsp; No algorithm deciding your reach based&nbsp;&nbsp; on engagement metrics you can't control. In an industry defined by exploitation,&nbsp;&nbsp; it sounded almost utopian. For&nbsp; a brief moment, it actually was.&nbsp; The early adopters were exactly who you would&nbsp; expect — adult content creators who had been&nbsp;&nbsp; burned by every other platform. They came&nbsp; because eighty percent felt revolutionary&nbsp;&nbsp; when you were used to getting nothing. By 2018,&nbsp; the platform had grown from modest beginnings to&nbsp;&nbsp; thirteen and a half million users and three&nbsp; hundred and forty-eight thousand creators.&nbsp; Then came the first ownership change.&nbsp; And everything began to shift. In 2018, Leonid Radvinsky bought a&nbsp; seventy-five percent controlling stake&nbsp;&nbsp; in OnlyFans' parent company, Fenix International,&nbsp;&nbsp; from the Stokely family. The acquisition&nbsp; price has never been publicly confirmed.&nbsp;&nbsp; What happened next changed OnlyFans in ways&nbsp; that would take years to fully understand.&nbsp; Radvinsky was, by any measure, one of the most&nbsp; unusual figures in the history of the modern&nbsp;&nbsp; internet. Born in Odesa, Ukraine, his family&nbsp; emigrated to a suburb outside Chicago when he was&nbsp;&nbsp; a child. He graduated from Northwestern University&nbsp; in 2002 with a degree in economics. By then he&nbsp;&nbsp; had already been operating websites for years — a&nbsp; network of adult content sites running through an&nbsp;&nbsp; Illinois business called Cybertania, which Forbes&nbsp; later documented as offering links to what the&nbsp;&nbsp; sites themselves described as "illegal pre-teen&nbsp; passwords" and other deeply disturbing material.&nbsp; Radvinsky's defence, and the nuanced truth of the&nbsp; matter, was that none of the links actually led to&nbsp;&nbsp; illegal content. The sites were scams — charging&nbsp; money for access to links that went nowhere. Which&nbsp;&nbsp; meant the business model was exploitative rather&nbsp; than criminal, a distinction that mattered legally&nbsp;&nbsp; but perhaps not morally. In 2004, Microsoft&nbsp; sued him for allegedly sending millions of&nbsp;&nbsp; deceptive emails to Hotmail users. The case&nbsp; was eventually dismissed for lack of proof.&nbsp; That same year he founded MyFreeCams, an adult&nbsp; webcam platform that would quietly generate&nbsp;&nbsp; enormous revenue over the following decade. By&nbsp; 2018, Radvinsky was a billionaire operating almost&nbsp;&nbsp; entirely in the shadows. He gave no interviews. He&nbsp; published no statements. A handful of photographs&nbsp;&nbsp; existed publicly. For one of the wealthiest men&nbsp; in the United States, he was nearly invisible.&nbsp; Banks had flagged his accounts in&nbsp; Suspicious Activity Reports over the years,&nbsp;&nbsp; citing unexplained transfers of millions&nbsp; of dollars and concerns about the nature of&nbsp;&nbsp; his business. Radvinsky and his defenders noted,&nbsp; accurately, that the adult industry is chronically&nbsp;&nbsp; over-flagged by banks that treat the entire sector&nbsp; as high-risk regardless of actual behaviour.&nbsp;&nbsp; The SARs proved nothing. But they painted&nbsp; a picture of a man who had operated for&nbsp;&nbsp; decades in the grey edges of the internet's&nbsp; most stigmatised industry — and who had now&nbsp;&nbsp; acquired one of its most powerful platforms. After the acquisition, the direction of&nbsp;&nbsp; OnlyFans shifted decisively toward adult content.&nbsp; Whatever the original vision of an all-creator&nbsp;&nbsp; platform had been, the market reality was&nbsp; clear: the money was in explicit material.&nbsp;&nbsp; The platform leaned into it. And the pandemic&nbsp; was about to pour rocket fuel onto everything. March 2020. The world locked down. Restaurants&nbsp; closed. Offices emptied. Gyms shut. Entire&nbsp;&nbsp; industries were switched off overnight. The&nbsp; economic devastation landed hardest on the&nbsp;&nbsp; people who could least afford it — service&nbsp; workers, hospitality staff, performers,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the vast informal economy of people who&nbsp; worked with their bodies and their presence,&nbsp;&nbsp; now suddenly unable to do either. OnlyFans was waiting.&nbsp; Between March and April 2020 alone,&nbsp; the user and creator base grew by&nbsp;&nbsp; seventy-five percent. Forty-eight hours&nbsp; after Beyoncé mentioned the platform in&nbsp;&nbsp; her remix of Megan Thee Stallion's "Savage,"&nbsp; traffic spiked by fifteen percent. The CEO&nbsp;&nbsp; reported two hundred thousand new users and&nbsp; between six and eight thousand new creators&nbsp;&nbsp; registering every single day. By December&nbsp; 2020, OnlyFans had eighty-five million users&nbsp;&nbsp; and more than a million creators, generating&nbsp; over two billion dollars in annual revenue.&nbsp; The cultural narrative that emerged was&nbsp; intoxicating. OnlyFans was liberation. It&nbsp;&nbsp; was the ultimate democratisation of income.&nbsp; You didn't need a studio, an agent, a label,&nbsp;&nbsp; or a boss. You needed a phone, a subscription, and&nbsp; the willingness to give people what they wanted.&nbsp;&nbsp; Newspapers ran pieces on teachers,&nbsp; nurses, and retail workers who had&nbsp;&nbsp; replaced their salaries with OnlyFans income&nbsp; in a matter of months. The word "empowerment"&nbsp;&nbsp; appeared in almost every article. Former&nbsp; stigma began dissolving in real time.&nbsp; And for some creators, the numbers were genuinely&nbsp; life-changing. A twenty-three-year-old woman named&nbsp;&nbsp; Savannah opened her account in January 2020 during&nbsp; job searching and by September had earned enough&nbsp;&nbsp; to buy her first house — something she had never&nbsp; believed would be possible. "I never, ever thought&nbsp;&nbsp; that I would be stable enough to buy a house,&nbsp; period, in my lifetime," she told TIME magazine.&nbsp;&nbsp; That story was real. That transformation was real. But it was not the average story. Not even close.&nbsp; The mathematical reality of OnlyFans was&nbsp; obscured almost completely by the cultural&nbsp;&nbsp; narrative. The platform ran then — and runs now&nbsp; — on a winner-takes-all model so extreme it makes&nbsp;&nbsp; most other industries look almost egalitarian&nbsp; by comparison. The top one percent of creators&nbsp;&nbsp; capture thirty-three percent of all revenue. The&nbsp; top ten percent capture seventy-three percent. The&nbsp;&nbsp; average creator in 2025 earned approximately&nbsp; one hundred and fifty to one hundred and&nbsp;&nbsp; eighty dollars per month. Annually, that&nbsp; is less than two thousand dollars. After a&nbsp;&nbsp; year of effort, content, marketing, and the&nbsp; psychological cost of performing intimacy&nbsp;&nbsp; for strangers — less than the cost of a&nbsp; single month's rent in most major cities.&nbsp; The stars who made millions were not just working&nbsp; hard. They were already famous, already followed,&nbsp;&nbsp; already trusted by audiences they had built over&nbsp; years on other platforms. OnlyFans didn't create&nbsp;&nbsp; their income — it provided a payment rail&nbsp; for an audience that already existed. For&nbsp;&nbsp; the millions of ordinary creators who arrived&nbsp; during the pandemic believing the success&nbsp;&nbsp; stories were representative, the experience was&nbsp; something else entirely: intense competition,&nbsp;&nbsp; rapidly declining average earnings, and the&nbsp; dawning realisation that they had joined a&nbsp;&nbsp; system whose economics worked brilliantly for a&nbsp; tiny elite and adequately for an invisible owner.&nbsp; In 2022 and 2023 alone, Leonid Radvinsky paid&nbsp; himself $810 million in dividends. In 2024,&nbsp;&nbsp; he extracted a further $701 million. In four&nbsp; years, he personally received approximately&nbsp;&nbsp; $1.8 billion from a platform he had bought by&nbsp; paying a family from Essex an undisclosed sum.&nbsp; That number — $1.8 billion — existed in direct&nbsp; relationship with every creator who opened an&nbsp;&nbsp; account believing the dream, posted their most&nbsp; vulnerable content, and earned $150 that month. OnlyFans sold itself on one promise above&nbsp; all others. The platform's language was&nbsp;&nbsp; explicit and consistent: authentic, direct&nbsp; relationships between creators and their fans.&nbsp;&nbsp; Not performance. Not content. Connection.&nbsp; Creators were not just selling videos.&nbsp;&nbsp; They were selling access to themselves. That promise was worth every dollar of&nbsp;&nbsp; premium pricing subscribers paid. And it was the&nbsp; promise that began quietly unravelling in 2024.&nbsp; In July of that year, Reuters published an&nbsp; investigation that revealed what had been&nbsp;&nbsp; an open secret inside the industry's most&nbsp; ambitious corners: a significant portion of&nbsp;&nbsp; OnlyFans interactions were not happening&nbsp; between creators and subscribers at all.&nbsp;&nbsp; They were being handled by "chatters" — paid&nbsp; employees, often based in the Philippines or&nbsp;&nbsp; Venezuela, hired by talent management agencies&nbsp; to impersonate creators in private messages.&nbsp;&nbsp; These chatters worked from detailed psychological&nbsp; profiles of each subscriber. They were trained&nbsp;&nbsp; specifically in what the lawsuits that&nbsp; followed would describe as techniques&nbsp;&nbsp; to "exploit emotional connections" and "prey&nbsp; on psychological biases and vulnerabilities."&nbsp; The business logic was straightforward.&nbsp; Top OnlyFans accounts received thousands&nbsp;&nbsp; of messages daily — a volume no single&nbsp; person could manage. Management agencies&nbsp;&nbsp; had emerged to handle the overflow. And those&nbsp; agencies had discovered, almost accidentally,&nbsp;&nbsp; that a skilled chatter operating a creator's&nbsp; account could generate significantly more&nbsp;&nbsp; income than the creator themselves, because&nbsp; a professional whose only job was to extract&nbsp;&nbsp; money from emotional men was better at it&nbsp; than someone also trying to create content,&nbsp;&nbsp; manage a social media presence, and&nbsp; maintain their mental health simultaneously.&nbsp; By 2024, AI had entered the equation. A chatbot&nbsp; called FlirtFlow, operated by a company called&nbsp;&nbsp; NEO Agency, was being used across dozens of&nbsp; accounts. It gathered personal information&nbsp;&nbsp; through small talk — where the subscriber was&nbsp; from, what was bothering him at work, what his&nbsp;&nbsp; dog's name was — and used that information&nbsp; to build a targeted emotional relationship&nbsp;&nbsp; designed to convert into payment requests. "You&nbsp; cannot directly jump into the typical 'Hey Daddy,&nbsp;&nbsp; tip me' stuff," its CEO explained to Reuters. "You&nbsp; have to start by really comprehending the fan."&nbsp; Comprehending the fan — in order to&nbsp; extract money from him more efficiently.&nbsp; Two class-action lawsuits were filed in the&nbsp; United States in 2024 and 2025. One plaintiff&nbsp;&nbsp; had joined specifically, his lawsuit stated, to&nbsp; have "friendly conversations with models and share&nbsp;&nbsp; photographs of his cooking creations." He became&nbsp; suspicious when messages began arriving with&nbsp;&nbsp; contradictions. He was right to be suspicious.&nbsp; He had never been talking to the creator at all.&nbsp; OnlyFans' terms of service explicitly prohibited&nbsp; the use of AI chatbots. The platform declined to&nbsp;&nbsp; answer Reuters' questions about their use. Its&nbsp; official position was that creators were legally&nbsp;&nbsp; responsible for any transaction with a subscriber.&nbsp; The platform, which took twenty percent of every&nbsp;&nbsp; single transaction those chatters and bots&nbsp; generated, accepted no liability for the fraud.&nbsp; That twenty percent cut — from billions&nbsp; of dollars of interactions, some portion&nbsp;&nbsp; of which were conducted by automated systems&nbsp; impersonating human beings — continued flowing&nbsp;&nbsp; to Fenix International. Registered in London.&nbsp; Owned in Florida. Invisible to almost everyone.&nbsp; Then, in August 2021, came the moment&nbsp; that revealed more about OnlyFans' real&nbsp;&nbsp; priorities than any press release ever could. The platform announced it would ban sexually&nbsp;&nbsp; explicit content entirely&nbsp; from October 2021 onwards.&nbsp; The creator community exploded in outrage. The&nbsp; platform that had built its entire existence on&nbsp;&nbsp; adult content — that had used adult creators&nbsp; as its founding community, its growth engine,&nbsp;&nbsp; its marketing vehicle — had without warning&nbsp; announced it was abandoning them to pursue&nbsp;&nbsp; mainstream investors and brand partnerships.&nbsp; The creators who had trusted OnlyFans with&nbsp;&nbsp; their income, their audience, their faces and&nbsp; bodies, discovered in a single press release that&nbsp;&nbsp; the platform's loyalty to them was conditional&nbsp; on a calculation that could change overnight.&nbsp; Six days later, OnlyFans reversed the decision. It&nbsp; claimed to have "secured assurances" from banking&nbsp;&nbsp; and payment partners. The reversal was celebrated&nbsp; as a creator victory. But what it actually&nbsp;&nbsp; demonstrated was something colder: the creators&nbsp; had won this round not because OnlyFans valued&nbsp;&nbsp; them — but because it needed them. The moment it&nbsp; found a path to mainstream acceptance that didn't&nbsp;&nbsp; require them, they would be expendable. That moment has not yet arrived. But&nbsp;&nbsp; the platform is clearly working toward it. By February 2026, OnlyFans&nbsp; operates at a scale that would&nbsp;&nbsp; have seemed impossible from a 2016 vantage point.&nbsp; Three hundred and seventy-seven million registered&nbsp; users. Four point six million creators. Seven&nbsp;&nbsp; point two billion dollars in gross fan spend in&nbsp; 2024 alone. An eight-billion-dollar valuation. The&nbsp;&nbsp; fifty-seventh most visited website in the&nbsp; United States. More traffic on mobile than&nbsp;&nbsp; almost any platform outside social media's&nbsp; inner circle. And at the centre of it&nbsp;&nbsp; all — a man worth approximately $7.8 billion&nbsp; according to Forbes, who gives no interviews,&nbsp;&nbsp; maintains near-total public silence, and has paid&nbsp; himself nearly two billion dollars in dividends&nbsp;&nbsp; over four years while remaining almost entirely&nbsp; unknown to the public whose money flows to him.&nbsp; The platform continues to pursue mainstream&nbsp; legitimacy through its free streaming service&nbsp;&nbsp; OFTV, through partnerships, and through carefully&nbsp; constructed narratives about the creator economy.&nbsp;&nbsp; Keily Blair, a lawyer by training, took over&nbsp; as CEO in 2023 and has navigated the platform's&nbsp;&nbsp; complex identity with measured professionalism.&nbsp; Tim Stokely, the founder, launched a new creator&nbsp;&nbsp; platform called Subs.com in May 2025, positioning&nbsp; it as a rival — a reminder that the man who built&nbsp;&nbsp; OnlyFans no longer owned it, and that the&nbsp; platform's current identity was shaped more&nbsp;&nbsp; by Radvinsky than by Stokely's original vision. The income gap has not narrowed. It has widened.&nbsp;&nbsp; The top one tenth of one percent of creators earn&nbsp; seventy-six percent of all platform revenue. The&nbsp;&nbsp; average creator earns $131 per month after&nbsp; platform fees. Many of those below the median&nbsp;&nbsp; earn less than minimum wage for the hours they&nbsp; invest — before accounting for the psychological&nbsp;&nbsp; cost of the work, the harassment from subscribers,&nbsp; the risk of content leaks, and the specific terror&nbsp;&nbsp; of having your most intimate moments permanently&nbsp; indexed on the internet under your face.&nbsp; Content leaks remain an endemic problem.&nbsp; In February 2020, up to four terabytes&nbsp;&nbsp; of stolen creator content spread across cloud&nbsp; platforms. Between December 2019 and June 2024,&nbsp;&nbsp; Reuters documented thirty cases in U.S. police&nbsp; and court records involving child sexual abuse&nbsp;&nbsp; material on the platform — over two hundred pieces&nbsp; of illegal content across thirty documented cases.&nbsp;&nbsp; OnlyFans has invested in verification systems&nbsp; and content moderation. The scale of the platform&nbsp;&nbsp; makes absolute protection impossible. The creators&nbsp; whose content is leaked bear the consequences. The&nbsp;&nbsp; platform takes its twenty percent regardless. And yet the numbers keep growing. Because the&nbsp;&nbsp; fundamental economic logic of OnlyFans — eighty&nbsp; percent to creators, direct relationship with&nbsp;&nbsp; audience, no algorithm controlling your reach&nbsp; — remains genuinely more creator-friendly than&nbsp;&nbsp; almost every alternative. The problem is not that&nbsp; the model is exploitative. The problem is that&nbsp;&nbsp; the model is good enough to keep people inside&nbsp; a system whose deeper mechanics are invisible&nbsp;&nbsp; to them until they are already dependent on it. A platform that sells authentic human connection&nbsp;&nbsp; while allowing AI to simulate that connection for&nbsp; profit. A platform that empowered creators with&nbsp;&nbsp; eighty percent of revenue while its reclusive&nbsp; owner extracted nearly two billion in personal&nbsp;&nbsp; dividends. A platform that reversed a ban on&nbsp; adult content not out of loyalty to its creators&nbsp;&nbsp; but because it needed more time to develop&nbsp; the exit strategy that wouldn't require them.&nbsp; That is what intimacy looks like&nbsp; when it has been industrialised. Here is what OnlyFans' story is actually about. It is not about sex. It is not about empowerment&nbsp;&nbsp; or exploitation — though it contains&nbsp; both. It is about what happens when human&nbsp;&nbsp; connection is placed inside a commercial&nbsp; infrastructure that has no mechanism for&nbsp;&nbsp; valuing it as anything other than a transaction. OnlyFans did not invent the commodification of&nbsp;&nbsp; intimacy. That is as old as civilisation. What it&nbsp; did was industrialise it at scale, and then add a&nbsp;&nbsp; layer of technology — first chatters, then&nbsp; AI — that progressively removed the human&nbsp;&nbsp; being from the interaction while maintaining&nbsp; the illusion of human presence. It built its&nbsp;&nbsp; entire business model on the promise of authentic&nbsp; connection, and then systematically introduced&nbsp;&nbsp; systems that made authenticity optional. The subscribers who sued in 2024 and 2025&nbsp;&nbsp; were not angry that they had paid for intimacy.&nbsp; They were angry that the intimacy they paid for&nbsp;&nbsp; was simulated. That the connection they&nbsp; thought they were buying was a product&nbsp;&nbsp; designed to resemble connection closely&nbsp; enough to generate continued payment.&nbsp; And here is the uncomfortable&nbsp; extension of that thought.&nbsp; Every platform you use that sells you "community,"&nbsp; "connection," or "authentic relationships" — and&nbsp;&nbsp; then monetises your engagement, your data,&nbsp; your attention, your emotional investment — is&nbsp;&nbsp; operating on a version of the same model. The&nbsp; difference between OnlyFans and every other major&nbsp;&nbsp; social platform is not that one sells connection&nbsp; and the others don't. It is that OnlyFans charges&nbsp;&nbsp; you directly for a service that other platforms&nbsp; extract from you indirectly through advertising.&nbsp; At least on OnlyFans, you&nbsp; know what you are paying for.&nbsp; On every other platform, the product is&nbsp; you. And the buyer is someone you never see.&nbsp; The question OnlyFans leaves us with is not&nbsp; whether selling intimacy is wrong. Human beings&nbsp;&nbsp; have always paid for connection — through&nbsp; gifts, through religion, through therapy,&nbsp;&nbsp; through every form of entertainment ever invented. The question is: what happens to a society when&nbsp;&nbsp; the systems we build to connect us are optimised&nbsp; not for connection — but for the revenue that the&nbsp;&nbsp; simulation of connection generates? We may be living in the answer. If that last thought unsettled you — it&nbsp; should. Because it was not meant to be about&nbsp;&nbsp; OnlyFans. It was meant to be about all of us. This channel exists for the people who want&nbsp;&nbsp; to understand how the systems around them&nbsp; actually work — not how they are presented,&nbsp;&nbsp; but what drives them, who profits from them,&nbsp; and what they cost the people inside them.&nbsp; If that is the kind of thinking you want&nbsp; more of, subscribe. Next week we go deep&nbsp;&nbsp; on another institution whose public image and&nbsp; private mechanics are separated by a distance&nbsp;&nbsp; that most people never examine. And if this video made you think&nbsp;&nbsp; of someone — a friend trying to make it as a&nbsp; creator, someone navigating the gig economy,&nbsp;&nbsp; someone who believes the success stories&nbsp; they were told about any platform — share&nbsp;&nbsp; it with them. Not as a warning. As a framework. Here is the question I want you to answer in the&nbsp;&nbsp; comments below: do you think there is a meaningful&nbsp; difference between a platform that sells simulated&nbsp;&nbsp; intimacy and one that sells simulated community?&nbsp; Or is the entire creator economy built on the&nbsp;&nbsp; same fundamental illusion? Tell me what you think.

- The Dark Economics of OnlyFans: Kind: captions Language: en There is a man who has made over 1.1 billion dollars from OnlyFans in a single year, and there is almost a 100% chance you have never heard his name. He does not do press. He does not post. He does not appear at technology conferences with a carefully rehearsed TED Talk about disruption. He filed his financial disclosures quietly in the United Kingdom in the kind of bureaucratic documents that most journalists walk right past. And he collected his money in the same silence. That alone would be a fascinating story. But here's the part that nobody talks about when they talk about OnlyFans. The platform that became the global symbol of creator empowerment, the one that supposedly let ordinary people cut out the middleman and finally get paid what they were worth, has a median creator income of less than 180 dollars per month. 180 dollars. That is not a typo. That is not me cherry-picking a bad month. That is the number. And the gap between that number and the 1.1 billion dollars at the top of the story tells you almost everything you need to know about who actually won when the creator economy arrived. So, let me take you through the whole thing. The founding myth, the invisible billionaire, the fake band that panicked an entire industry, and the structural reality that every creator on every platform is eventually going to run into whether they know it or not. Because this is not really a story about adult content. It is a story about who controls the internet and how they use that control when it counts. And the answer, as always, is not the person you thought. OnlyFans launched in 2016. Tim Stokely, a British entrepreneur in his early 30s, built it in London on a premise that was genuinely simple and genuinely powerful. Creators could post content behind a paywall. Fans would pay a monthly subscription to access it. OnlyFans would take 20%. The creator would keep 80, and the whole thing would operate without the algorithm-driven starvation that was already making Instagram and YouTube feel like a second job that never paid enough. That pitch landed at exactly the right cultural moment. The gig economy was booming. Trust in traditional employment was eroding. And the idea that you could monetize your audience directly rather than renting access to them from a platform felt genuinely revolutionary. The marketing practically wrote itself. No middleman. No algorithm gatekeeping your income. Just you and your audience. Yep, you heard that right. No middleman. They said that with a straight face. For the first couple of years, OnlyFans grew steadily but not explosively. Fitness trainers used it. Musicians used it. Chefs used it. The platform was designed to be content-agnostic. And for a while, that was mostly true. But there is a reason that by 2019, the vast majority of the platform's traffic was coming from a very specific category of content. Adult creators discovered that OnlyFans solved a problem that had plagued their industry for decades, payment processing. Major platforms refused to touch them. Banks treated them like a liability. Credit card companies applied extra friction at every step. OnlyFans, almost by accident, had built infrastructure that worked for them. And when adult creators moved in, they brought their audiences with them. And the growth curve went nearly vertical. Then, 2020 happened. The pandemic shut down the entire economy in a matter of weeks. And a lot of people who had never considered OnlyFans started looking at it very seriously. The platform added over 200,000 new creators in April of 2020 alone. Mainstream celebrities started joining to stay connected to fans. The media wrote breathless profiles about former waitresses and laid-off nurses who were making five figures a month from their bedrooms. The platform was processing billions of dollars in transactions and growing faster than almost any consumer technology company in history. At its peak, OnlyFans was reportedly handling more transactions per day than some mid-size banks. Here is what I want you to keep in the back of your mind as I tell you the rest of this story because it is going to matter in a few minutes. None of this growth happened in a vacuum. And the platform that was supposedly liberating creators from the tyranny of Big Tech was at the exact same time becoming extremely dependent on a small number of very powerful financial institutions. That dependency is not an accident. It is a design feature of how money moves on the internet. And it is the reason that what happened in August of 2021 was not a surprise to anyone who had been paying attention. But before I get to August of 2021, I need to tell you about the man who was quietly collecting 20% of everything the entire time. Leonid Radvinsky was born in Ukraine and grew up in the United States. He is, by almost all accounts, extraordinarily private. There are almost no photographs of him in circulation. He rarely speaks to journalists. His public profile is close to nonexistent for someone sitting on a fortune of that size. What we do know comes largely from court records, regulatory filings, and the kind of investigative journalism that involves reading extremely boring documents for a very long time. And what those documents reveal is a background that is, let us say, textured. Before OnlyFans, Radvinsky was associated with MyFreeCams, one of the largest adult webcam platforms in the world. That connection alone is not scandalous. The adult entertainment industry is a legal industry. But the fuller picture of his early career involves companies that were sued and investigated for spam campaigns targeting users of adult websites. The FTC went after several of these operations in the 2010s. And Radvinsky's name appeared in the orbit of some of those cases, though not as a defendant. He was apparently operating adjacent to the lines rather than across them. Which is a skill, a very profitable skill, as it turns out. Radvinsky acquired a controlling stake in OnlyFans in 2018. Reports suggest he paid something in the range of 10 million dollars for what became a 75% ownership position. 10 million dollars for a platform that would go on to process over 5 billion dollars in a single year. That is either the best deal in the history of digital media or a number that makes you want to sit very quietly in a dark room and think about the choices you have made in your life. I am going to guess it is both. Now, here's the part of the story where I need to introduce a second character because the real drama of OnlyFans does not come from Radvinsky alone. It comes from the institutions that were always quietly holding a gun to the entire platform's head without anyone really talking about it openly. I am talking about Visa and Mastercard. And to understand what they did to OnlyFans, you need to understand what happened to a different company first. In December of 2020, The New York Times published an investigation into Pornhub, the world's largest adult content platform, documenting the presence of videos depicting child sexual abuse and non-consensual content. The piece was devastating. Within days of publication, Mastercard and Visa announced they were suspending their services to Pornhub entirely. Pornhub, which at the time was processing an enormous volume of transactions, was functionally cut off from the global payment system overnight. The platform was forced to delete millions of videos and implement sweeping verification systems. Its parent company, MindGeek, eventually imploded under the weight of lawsuits and regulatory pressure. This was a genuinely important piece of journalism about genuinely serious crimes. But the financial industry's response to it set a precedent that had nothing to do with crimes and everything to do with risk tolerance. Because what Visa and Mastercard discovered in that moment was that they had an enormous amount of leverage over any platform that depended on their networks, and that using that leverage generated almost no political blowback when the target was the adult content industry. They filed it away. And they kept watching. OnlyFans knew what had happened to Pornhub. They watched very carefully. They had already spent years building relationships with payment processors and banks, positioning themselves as the responsible alternative, the platform with verification systems and content moderation and a commitment to keeping everything legal and consensual. They had put real infrastructure behind those claims. And for a while, that positioning worked. But by mid-2021, the banks were getting nervous again. The platform was growing so fast that its risk profile was becoming impossible to ignore. The volume of transactions was enormous. The reputational exposure for any bank associated with explicit content at that scale was starting to feel uncomfortable in the boardroom. And some of Visa and Mastercard's largest institutional shareholders were beginning to ask questions. So, in August of 2021, OnlyFans announced that it would ban sexually explicit content starting in October. The announcement landed like a grenade. Creators who had built their entire income on the platform, in some cases for years, suddenly faced the possibility of losing everything with two months' notice. The press coverage was immediate and overwhelming. Politicians who had never heard of OnlyFans were suddenly being asked about it. Former sex workers were giving interviews about financial devastation. The platform's own creators were organizing, threatening mass exodus, posting tearful videos about the lives they had built and what this announcement meant for them. And then, 6 days later, OnlyFans reversed the decision entirely. They announced that they had received assurances from their banking partners and that the ban would not go forward. Explicit content would remain on the platform. 6 days. The whole crisis, start to finish, lasted 6 days. Here is the thing about those 6 days that most coverage missed entirely. OnlyFans did not reverse course because of the creator backlash. The backlash was real and it was loud and it generated enormous press coverage, but that is not what moved the needle. The reversal happened because OnlyFans had been in conversations with payment processors and financial institutions throughout the entire period and those conversations ultimately produced whatever assurances the platform needed to continue. The creators did not win. The payment processors made a business decision. Those are two very different things and confusing them is how you end up believing a story about creator power that is not actually about creator power at all. What those 6 days really demonstrated was the fundamental architecture of digital financial infrastructure. It is not flat. It is distributed. It is not democratic. It is a series of choke points controlled by a small number of institutions and every platform that processes money for adult content, legal or otherwise, operates at the permanent pleasure of those institutions. The creators on OnlyFans were not free. They were free until Visa decided they were not and then they had 6 days of uncertainty before Visa decided they could go back to work. That is not the creator economy. That is a landlord. Let me tell you something about the economics underneath all of this that makes it even worse. The OnlyFans pitch is built on the idea that creators keep 80% and they do. That number is real, but 80% of what? The platform has over 200 million registered users. Sounds enormous, but the number of those users who have ever paid for anything is somewhere around 1 to 2%. Maybe less. The active paying fan base is actually quite small relative to the registered user count. And the revenue from that paying fan base is distributed in a way that looks almost nothing like the egalitarian promise in the marketing. Studies of creator economy platforms consistently find the same basic pattern. The top 1% of creators capture somewhere between 30 and 40% of all the revenue. The top 10% capture somewhere between 70 and 80%. The remaining 90% of creators are splitting the leftover 20 to 30% among themselves. Run those numbers and you get a median income of less than $180 per month. That is the real number. Not the headline number about the creator making six figures. Not the viral story about the teacher who quit her job. The median. The number that describes what a typical creator actually makes when you strip away the survivorship bias and look at the whole distribution. And here is where the 20% take becomes interesting in a way that should make you uncomfortable. Because 20% of $5 billion in annual transactions is $1 billion. That is the platform's gross revenue before any costs. A significant chunk of that flows upward and we know it flows upward because of the financial filings in the United Kingdom, the ones that almost nobody read, which showed that in the fiscal year ending November 2021, Leonid Radvinsky received dividends of over $1.1 billion in 1 year. From a platform he purchased for approximately $10 million 3 years earlier. Take a moment with that. $1.1 billion in dividends in dividends in 1 year from a platform that was simultaneously publishing press releases about supporting creator financial independence. I want to be precise here because precision matters. Radvinsky did not do anything illegal. The platform operates legally. The 20% cut is disclosed. The terms of service are public. Nobody forced any creator to sign up. All of that is true. And it is also true that the structure of this platform, like the structure of almost every platform in the creator economy, is designed to extract the maximum possible value from the tail of the distribution while using the success stories of the top creators as marketing for the people at the bottom. The few people at the top are real. Their success is real. And they are also the bait. This is where it gets genuinely interesting and I want you to stay with me because what I am about to tell you is the part that actually changes how you see not just OnlyFans, but the entire framework of the creator economy. There is a concept in economics called a two-sided market. A two-sided market is a platform that serves two distinct groups of users simultaneously. In this case, creators and fans and extracts value from the interaction between them. The platform does not produce content. The platform does not produce demand. The platform simply sits in the middle, facilitates the transaction, and takes a cut. This is in structure almost identical to every middleman arrangement in the history of commerce. The record label that controlled distribution and took 80% of an artist's earnings. The literary agent that owned the rights to a writer's catalog. The gallery that controlled access to collectors and took 40% of every sale. The OnlyFans pitch was that it was different, that the 20% was fundamentally unlike the 80% that a record label took. Because at least you were keeping 80. And mathematically, yes, 80 is more than 20. But the label also provided studios, producers, marketing, distribution, and radio promotion. OnlyFans provides a website and payment processing. The question is not what percentage they take. The question is what they do with it. Who accumulates the power as the platform grows and who is expendable if the business model changes? And the answer to who is expendable showed up in August of 2021 when the platform was willing to throw its entire creator base under a bus in the name of banking relationships and then pulled them back only when the banking relationships stabilized. The creators were not the customers. They were the inventory. Here is the pattern interrupt that I think is the most important data point in this entire story. OnlyFans is not unique. The dynamics I just described are not specific to adult content or to this particular platform. They are the operating model of the entire creator economy. YouTube takes 45% of ad revenue from creators, not 20%. Spotify pays artists an average of between $0.003 and $0.005 per stream, which is a number so small it is almost theoretical. The App Store takes 30% of every purchase a developer makes through it. Substack takes 10%. Patreon takes up to 12%. Every single platform in the creator economy is a two-sided market with a toll booth in the middle. And the toll booth operator is the one who gets rich while the creators are busy arguing about whether the algorithm is fair. The genius of the OnlyFans positioning and the reason it worked so effectively as a brand is that 20% genuinely felt different in context. Against the background of record labels and Spotify and YouTube, 20% sounded like a liberation movement. The entire marketing strategy was built on comparison to worse alternatives, rather than on the question of whether any percentage taken by a passive intermediary is justified when the creators are producing all of the value. The answer to that question, if you ask it plainly, is not comfortable for any platform that has ever published a press release about empowering creators. Now, let me bring the payment processor subplot back in because it does not end with the August 2021 reversal. The resolution of that crisis was not a resolution. It was a temporary ceasefire. The fundamental dynamic, which is that OnlyFans is dependent on payment processors that can pull their services at any time and for any reason, did not change. What changed was that Visa and MasterCard decided, for the moment, that the relationship was worth maintaining. But they could change their minds again. And they know it and OnlyFans knows it. And that knowledge shapes every significant decision the platform makes about content policy, creator policy, and financial infrastructure. This is why, despite the reversal, OnlyFans has continued to quietly tighten its content policies in the years since. There have been incremental expansions of prohibited content categories, new verification requirements, new restrictions on certain types of transactions. None of these changes generated the same press coverage as the August announcement because none of them were announced as dramatically. They were rolled out gradually through policy updates buried in terms of service documents, the way most significant corporate decisions affecting creators are actually communicated. Not with a press release, with a checkbox you have to click before you can log in. The creators who depend most on the platform, the ones who have built their entire income structure around it, are the ones least able to push back against these incremental changes because leaving is expensive. Not just emotionally expensive, though it is that, too, but financially expensive in a very literal way. A creator who has spent 2 years building a subscriber base on OnlyFans has built that subscriber base on OnlyFans. The subscribers are on OnlyFans. The payment infrastructure is on OnlyFans. The creator's profile, built through years of marketing and community cultivation, lives on OnlyFans. Moving that subscriber base to a different platform requires convincing every paying customer to follow you somewhere new, and research on subscriber churn in this context consistently shows that a significant percentage of subscribers do not make that move. The platform knows this. It is not accidental that subscriber contact information is not portable off the platform. This is the mechanism through which platforms accumulate power over time. Not by taking more at the moment of creation, but by making the cost of leaving grow larger and larger the longer you stay. In the economics literature, this is called lock-in. And it is one of the most reliable ways to build a durable monopoly. You do not cage the creators directly. You just make the cage feel, over time, like the only place where the food is. Revinsky, who understands digital media economics in a way that most tech executives pretend to, built this lock-in intentionally. The platform's tools for building subscriber relationships, the messaging systems, the subscription tiers, the tip mechanisms, all of them create intimacy between creator and fan that is mediated entirely through OnlyFans infrastructure. Every message sent is a message sent on OnlyFans. Every payment is a payment through OnlyFans. Every relationship is, at its foundation, a relationship that OnlyFans could theoretically terminate at any time. That is not a bug. That is the product. Let me tell you something about what the top creators actually experience because it complicates the story in a way that matters. The women and men at the very top of the OnlyFans income distribution are genuinely making extraordinary money. There are creators who earn over $1 million a year from the platform. There are a handful who earn significantly more. For them, the 20% cut is painful in the abstract, but manageable in practice. Because 20% of a very large number is still a very large number. These creators have real agency. They have legal and financial teams. Some of them have negotiated custom arrangements with the platform. Their experience of OnlyFans is categorically different from the experience of the creator making $180 a month. But here's what I want you to notice about those top creators. They are the ones who appear in the press coverage. They are the ones whose success stories are amplified by the platform's marketing, explicitly or implicitly. They are the ones whose lives and incomes validate the premise that OnlyFans is a path to financial independence. And they represent, by number, something close to 1% of the creator base. The other 99% are subsidizing the platform's growth and Revinsky's dividends while the marketing tells them they are on the road to the same destination. This is survivorship bias operating at industrial scale. And it works because the human brain is not built to assess probability distributions. We hear one story about the teacher who made $300,000 in in a year on OnlyFans, and we think the category's real, the path is achievable, the platform is what it says it is. We do not intuitively adjust for the fact that for every one of those stories, there are thousands of creators who never broke $500 a month and quietly gave up. The platform knows this, of course. Every platform knows this. The creator economy as a category depends on this psychological feature of human cognition, the way that a visible success can make an invisible failure seem like a personal shortcoming rather than a structural inevitability. It is the same mechanism that makes people buy lottery tickets. Not because they have done the probability math, but because somewhere nearby someone did win. And that presence in the cultural environment feels like evidence of personal possibility rather than statistical noise. What happened to Tim Stokely is its own small story within this larger one. He stepped down as CEO in 2021, just months after the ban and reversal drama. The official communications framed it as a planned transition. The timing, immediately following the most chaotic period in the platform's history and the year of Revinsky's $1.1 billion dividend extraction, raised some eyebrows. He collected his share of the proceeds from the years of growth, took his exit, and moved on. The man who had founded the platform and built its public brand disappeared from the story at almost exactly the moment when the financial reality of what had been built became impossible to hide in the press coverage. New leadership took over. The platform continued to grow. Revinsky continued to collect. The payment processors continued to hold the leverage. And the content moderation policies continued to quietly tighten, one policy update at a time, in the way that large platforms adjust themselves when they are trying to become respectable enough to survive the next banking conversation without another 6-day crisis. The broader implication of all of this, and I am not going to spell it out for you because you do not need me to, is that the creator economy did not eliminate the middleman. It created a new one with better marketing. The old middleman, whether it was a record label, a publisher, or a talent agency, was visible. You could see the contract. You could see the entity taking the cut. The new middleman is a platform. And platforms have spent an enormous amount of money and cultural energy convincing creators and consumers alike that they are not middlemen at all, that they are neutral infrastructure, that they are on your side, that the alternative to them is somehow worse. And in some specific ways, on some specific dimensions, they are right. 20% is less than 80%, but $1.1 billion in personal dividends from a platform built on the labor of creators who are making less than $180 a month is not a revolution. It is the same extraction rebranded. The payment processors are still there. They still hold the lever. They demonstrated that explicitly in 2021, and they have not relinquished it. The next time a bank's institutional shareholders start asking questions about reputational exposure, or the next time a politician decides that this industry is a useful target, or the next time a single piece of investigative journalism shifts the public conversation the way the Pornhub piece did in 2020, OnlyFans will face the same choice it faced in August of 2021, and the creators will have 6 days or fewer to figure out what that means for their rent. The man with $1.1 billion in dividends will be fine either way. He does not give interviews. He does not post. He will not be at the press conference. He'll be somewhere quiet, reading the filing, and moving on. There is a creator right now, probably several thousand of them, who signed up for OnlyFans this month because they saw a headline about someone making $100,000 a year from their phone. They believe they are cutting out the middleman. They believe they are building something that belongs to them. They are building it on someone else's infrastructure, using someone else's payment processing, reaching an audience they cannot contact if the platform decides they cannot, keeping 80% of a number that, for most of them, will never be large enough to matter, and generating a fraction of a fraction of a fraction of the $1.1 billion that moved quietly upward in a single year to a man whose name they have almost certainly never heard. The platform that promised to eliminate the middleman made one man a billionaire. The creators made the content. The fans paid the subscriptions. Visa and MasterCard held the gun. And Leonid Revinsky collected the money. The new boss is the same as the old boss. He just has a better landing page.

Those are not self- serving. Those don't feel good on the surface, though they feel good in a different way, in a very fulfilling way. And I can think now like what, you know, I I know what it's like to live a life of I keep calling it degeneracy. It's the only word I kind of have for it, but I know what it's like to enter into those lifestyles. And if I could choose it today, I would not. Though it may be easy to do so, I want to talk more about your faith and how this kind of transformed you. So, you you read this CS Lewis book. You're dating this guy. How does that foray into something bigger? >> So, obviously, I get pregnant and I have a baby. So most women will tell you that changes you just it changes you a lot. Being a mother is just the most amazing thing. So that obviously I went through physical changes, emotional changes, lifestyle changes. Um and as I started to enter into Christianity, go to church, I started to explore Catholicism a little bit more. Um so it was changing me in ways that it's kind of hard to describe. I mean, I was struggling with a lot of what I would call trauma, um, mental illness, like PTSD. I was on meds, all these things, all in my 20s, in a lot of therapy. Suddenly, a lot of that just started to go away. Um, anxiety was disappearing, triggers were disappearing, relationships were easier. I mean, the guy, um, the father of my child, my partner and I, we had a really hard time when our baby was born. Like, we could not get along. We were fighting all the time. Um, I was lashing out. It was it was very difficult. And the more I turned to God and the more I accepted and kind of humbled myself, just things started to get so much easier. I just accepted my duties as a partner to this man, as a mother, and my life just started to change in ways it's it's hard to describe. It just things got better, things got easier, more peaceful, just problems started to solve themselves. Um, so I just kept leaning into it. I got more devoted to the church. Eventually I conferred to Catholicism which I can talk about but that's the best way I can describe it is just a transformation. Did you feel like everything kind of clicked? Like I I guess the the the real question is do you wish you had found God sooner or do you feel like all of that lostness and searching kind of was necessary? Of course I wish I found God sooner because uh who wants to suffer longer than they have to? So, I think you're right in the fact that I probably needed to go through a lot of that. I think what people tend to say is God finds you, meets you where you are, meets you where you can be. So, I I am not sure I would have been open to God until I was, you know, I think if someone some guy I was dating had been like, "Come to church with me in my early 20s," I would have just dumped him. Like, I don't think I was ready for that. Um, so, you know, I I think there's a reason I got pregnant. I think there's a reason I had my beautiful daughter. Um, you know, and even looking back and all the ways God was in my life before I even accepted him and and how he kept me safe in certain situations and how, you know, I I met certain people or certain things, how I woke up one day wanting to be sober. It's actually just incredible. So, of course, I wish I had the peace I have now, but I'm not sure I could have. It's just I I would at this point in my life, it would be hard to be convinced of that just based on results and how good my life is and how great I feel. >> Yeah. What can you can do in the first week and then how to move past all of that and start to rebuild, you know, your career, get that next job, put yourself in the right frame of mind to actually go out and make the the time between your separation from one company and the the time you onboard to a new company as as small as possible. So step one, first of all, if you're in the moment or you think it's coming and you know you you get this phantom email, this random email on one day from your boss and their your HR person is is on copy. Don't panic. Take a deep breath. Go into it. It it'll be awkward and very uncomfortable. I promise you. Get through it and try not to react. Try not to react with, you know, emotion. Just kind of calmly take it in. And I know that's super hard, but I've seen people actually get fired out of these meetings because they have behaved aggressively, angry, they've actually become violent. So if you know that those these are triggers for you and you can do anything just to push that down, hear what the people have to say, which will be wildly scripted, get through the conversation, and then exit with as much dignity as possible. The emotions are going to hit right following this meeting. You're going to hit with be hit with fear, shame, perhaps even some relief, especially if you've known this has been coming or even that rage, right? That rage and and that is all normal. And I would say it's it's what you do with it that is an important part. Feel everything you need to feel. Pop open a bottle of wine. Don't get stuck there, though. Please do not get stuck there. But just don't also react publicly. Don't just stay away from social social media. If you do need a social media outlet, go on Reddit. You've got a an anonymous profile with some random, you know, screen name and vent as much as you want there. That that's probably the safest space publicly to go. But don't go on any of the big platforms and start venting because I don't think that will do you any favors down the road. Okay, I said this before. You're in the meeting and maybe if you're fortunate, they are putting a severance agreement in front of you. That's a good thing that they are offering you some parting gifts, if you will. But I would say to you, do not sign it in that moment. If you have a good HR person actually facilitating this conversation, they would not let you sign it in the moment. So, take it home. You are absolutely entitled to have an attorney look it over, read it over. It's super boring. It's probably a boilerplate severance agreement um that they are given to everybody that's impacted so they can say it's been consistently done. But look at what they're giving you that the weeks of pay, your your any PTO payout, you know, COBRA payments, whatever it is. Make sure you understand it fully. And if you don't, call that HR person back, ask some questions so you know what you're signing. When you do sign that, you are signing away to get that money or those benefits or whatever it is in that agreement. To get that money, you're signing away your right to come sue that company later. And that's an important distinction. So that's why that company is going to want to get that signature from you because you're waving your right to come back to them with a lawsuit for for whatever you know the circumstances is. You may also be signing away your rights for an unemployment claim. So definitely have an attorney look at that and answer those types of questions. All right, this one next thing I'm going to say is again in the moment um or maybe shortly thereafter is see if you can get the termination in writing. It'll probably be some very standard letter if at all they get. I think it's 50/50 if they're going to actually give you something. So, that you may need and depends on the state or or what have you. When you go to apply for unemployment, you may need that letter. Really understand when your last paycheck is coming. Especially if you're in California, they're going to require like a quick pay. Make sure you understand when that le next paycheck is coming. And is it coming as a hard check or is it, you know, old school or is it coming as a, you know, a direct deposit, which I assume you've been on before. But make sure you have all that clarity when you know if you've got um PTO uh paid time off hours that are coming to you when will that be will that be included in your in your final paycheck or is that going to come separately. So make sure you understand when your last day is all that type of you know housekeeping stuff. And in the moment even if you forget to ask go back and ask that HR person that's what they're there for. They will clearly walk through you through the whole conversation again because a lot of time you're not able to listen in that moment. So they're happy to walk you through the conversation again to make sure you fully understand what you're signing and and all that type of stuff. Get your stuff and get out as quickly as possible. Don't react negatively or aggressively or or with god forbid violence because that is just going to make the situation much much worse. If you've got other materials like a laptop or or whatever you have maybe for your home setup that you have to return, make sure you're returning those because I'm not going to lie, we've sent the cops to some people's houses to get some stuff back. That could actually put a severance agreement in jeopardy as well. Okay, so 24 hours has gone by, you've marinated it, you've talked to a friend, maybe you've popped open a bottle of wine. Again, don't get stuck there. The next thing, the first thing you should do is go and apply for unemployment benefits because it is a bit of a lengthy process. There's lots of stuff, you know, you may need to be able to answer. And then if you do have a severance agreement, that's going to play a part in that because you apply for the unemployment benefits and they're going to say, "Did you get a severance agreement when you were terminated?" If the answer is yes, that may affect the timing of when you are eligible to receive any unemployment payments. Every state is different. So, the first thing you need to do is get through that. Make sure that that process is started so then you can get into whatever process it is and know when that unemployment payment is going to come to you. Most companies that are doing layoffs will usually not contest the unemployment claim and therefore it'll just kind of go right through and you'll start receiving payments. If you've been fired for cause um for you know something you did in the workplace, let's say it's harassment, right? the company may contest, hey, this person was fired for this and we're going to come to a hearing to contest this unemployment charge. That may happen and that may delay again you getting any unemployment benefits. So, keep all those things in mind. Know the process. Start it as soon as you can. The day after you get fired is my recommendation. Okay. So, the other thing people stress about and sometimes panic about is they're unaware of when their health benefits end as a result of their separation from the company, especially people with multiple dependents, kids, or whatever, whatever the case may be. So, the good news is if you get laid off, say the first of the month, as an example, your benefits are going to be automatically effective through the end of that month. So, that gives you some time to go wrap up some doctor's appointments or what have you. Now, COBRA, and I don't remember the AC acronym, but what it is, it's the government's way of allowing you to continue the benefits you had at your former employer. And so, that will come within about 60 days. You can go on the website. I'll actually link it here. So, you can go on and see what the application process is, see what the rates you would pay. I'm not going to lie, it's expensive, but you don't want to go without benefits. The other thing you could do is, you know, look into the, you know, the Affordable Care Act and and kind of what that marketplace looks like for some different um health care options. If you do not elect Cobra, meaning say yes, I want it within 60 days of your separation, then it's going to go away and it won't be available to you. All right? You know, here's a tale of of what not to do. And um this involves a former executive that um I used to work with. What she missed doing, and this was in a retirement situation, but it's applicable to a um termination or separation from the company as well, is she didn't pay attention to the deadlines on stock options or or you know, stock grants, all that type of stuff. So, they've all got different kind of, you know, especially when you leave a company, what the what is vested, all these type of things, right? So, she left a million bucks on the table because she missed some of these deadlines and that actually there was nothing the company could do because it was managed by an overarching plan. You're you're showing up as your best self and you're you're putting forth the energy that they want to hire you. - Surviving a Violent Relationship | Can Ask Meh?: Kind: captions Language: en I felt a lot of anger and shame because I pride myself on being this really assertive and independent woman yet behind the scenes I was being abused so badly. I think there were many moments throughout the six-month relationship that I wanted to leave and I did try to leave. Before I had this experience I always thought that like if a guy ever hits me, that’s it it’s the end of the relationship. I never thought that I would be someone in a violent relationship and could not leave. Throughout the relationship there were moments where he was physically rough with me but I think there were three major incidents where he was basically really violent to me and the final one was the one in the news. The one where he broke so many of my bones I was in the hospital for three weeks I had a brain hemorrhage and things like that. So after that incident he just did not contact me anymore. There was just no more communication after that. I like to think it was a state-mandated breakup. Obviously if someone keeps hitting you or is nasty towards you eventually you will think that it’s pointless you just wouldn’t stay on. But in violent relationships or abusive relationships there are great moments too. There are really high moments where you will feel really happy. With him I always felt like I could be myself I did not have to hide who I am. So people need to understand that there is a high and there’s a low and even though the low seems like absolutely horrible the high could make people stay, it’s like a drug. It’s no different from any drug out there. ‘How could he do this to me?’ Yeah, that was my first thought. I went to the mirror and looked at myself I was like ‘whoa’. It was so... my face was so horribly disfigured. It was all a tangled mess of blood and I was still thinking ‘Oh you know what, if he goes to prison I will still wait for him when he comes out’. Because like I said it was a breakup without a breakup, it was a state-mandated breakup and we could not contact each other. So it was very painful I lost someone who was a great part of my life even though it was full of chaos and everything. I think I was like crying most of the time. I was also spending my time reading a lot. So I think that sort of helped and I slowly came to the realisation that this person does not care for me at all has zero interest for my welfare and would not risk anything (in order to) protect himself. Meaning he would not even come to apologise or visit me whatsoever. I slowly realised through therapy that actually, maybe, I didn’t really have a high self-worth at all. That was a hard pill to swallow. So definitely the impending court case was a big push factor for me to prepare myself because I knew that it was something that I could not back out of because the police were involved. I had three years to prepare for the court case. So throughout that I was already embarking on my journey of healing. I am not just doing this for myself. There are a lot of people out there getting abused also I cannot just go out there and be ‘whatever’ about it. It built me up, I would say if anything at all, it just built me up. Physically, I am probably still in the healing process because even just last year I had to go for surgery for my hand (injury) again. Psychologically, I would say I am in a much better place. Healing is not linear because there are days where you know I feel compassion for my ex, I feel like I can forgive him already. Next thing I know, like some days after the rage would surface again, I would be angry again. I think I was fortunate enough I rarely have flashbacks or nightmares. So, I think when I was in the hospital somehow I was being referred to PAVE (Family Violence Specialist Centre) to go through this group counselling with a number of women who were in a similar situation as me. I mean I didn’t want to go because I still had this misconception of women in violent relationships. Even though I was one, I thought they are very meek, feeble women not assertive and they are really just afraid of everything. I eventually went and had a shock of my life because these women were not what I expected. There were people who were smart, funny, brilliant, and they had great jobs. So at PAVE when I heard those stories of how these women were being abused I was so angry, I was so furious. How could any man treat a woman like that? Then after a while, it struck me how come I am so angry for these women but I was not angry for myself at the way I was being treated? So it all boils down to a lack of self-love. So that made me see the light, in a way. After that relationship I actually did not have fear that I would meet another abusive partner. I have become very wary and very cautious and I would say much more adept than before at spotting red flags. I was not even looking out for a relationship and actually I feel that’s something that I could never imagine myself to be you know, five years ago. I’ve befriended myself. I’m my own best friend. I am happy doing things alone. There’re so many things to do alone. I am just really happy. Be kind and gentle to yourself the way you are kind and gentle to others. I think I had a lot of compassion for people I could empathise with people who were in a lot of pain but when I was suffering myself I had no self-compassion, I was always very harsh on myself. I think what is important for these victims to know is that I feel like logically they already know a lot of these things they know that this is not healthy and this is not good. But they can’t help but feel so connected to that person. After the victim gets abused heavily there’s a small window of opportunity where an outsider can come in and shake them up and (tell them to) not go back. But the person helping must not have expectations and must not judge the victims. Instead of being so quick to pass advice telling them you should break up, no no. Be there for them, listen to them. Maybe just ask questions. I feel like asking questions is the best way to snap people out of their delusion. Ask them maybe “If he really loves you, why would he hit you?” I guess more awareness could be raised (on where) victims of violence can go to. What I hope people can take away from hearing my story is that abusive relationships are very common even if it’s not physical. Some people may not even realise that they are in an abusive relationship because it’s so normalised to them. Don’t be afraid of being alone. It’s better to be in solitude and learn more about yourself than be in a relationship that’s not good for you out of fear of being alone. If this did not happen I would not be who I am today I would not be where I am today and I am very content with that. So that’s the best thing that came out of it all. I am happy with myself. Over the past three years there has been a steady increase in the number of family violence cases. Rachel eventually got the help she needed but only after she was badly hurt. These are some of the common red flags of an unsafe relationship. But red flags can also appear in less obvious forms. If you feel lousy about yourself because of what your partner says and does to you or you feel like you’re walking on eggshells or have to report your every move. Remember, love shouldn’t hurt. If you, or anyone you know, are in danger talk to a professional. Here are some resources and helplines. There is also a Signal for Help that you can use, especially over video calls.
